current_fact.fact_text: On May 20, 1217, the Second Battle of Lincoln saw William Marshal lead English royal forces to defeat Prince Louis of France and rebellious barons. Chroniclers dubbed it the "Fair of Lincoln" because of the vast amount of loot taken, marking a turning point that helped secure the young king Henry III’s throne.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

current_fact.match_reason: A decisive medieval city battle sits alongside an episode about a modern industrial city’s identity, both exploring how places are forged through conflict, class, and collective stories.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1967, the Popular Movement of the Revolution was founded in what would later be called Zaire, becoming the single ruling party under Mobutu Sese Seko. The party fused politics with personality cult, turning ideology into a kind of mass performance that saturated daily life.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A party built around a leader’s carefully crafted public image parallels an episode about alter‑egos and performed identities, though one is steeped in power and the other in art.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1956, as part of Operation Redwing, the United States dropped its first airborne hydrogen bomb over Bikini Atoll. The explosion lit up the Pacific sky and scarred a coral paradise, leaving behind radioactive lagoons and a cautionary tale about the scale of human-made fire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A nuclear test is the most extreme kind of man‑made "shock," mirroring the episode’s theme of raw electrical power, but here flipped into a somber reminder of destructive energy.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1985, Radio Martí began transmitting from the United States toward Cuba, beaming news and commentary over shortwave frequencies. The station turned radio waves into a contested border, part information lifeline, part instrument of propaganda in the long, crackling Cold War.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A politically charged radio signal crossing the sea connects to Sedna’s own idea of radio as a journey, carrying voices and stories over invisible paths.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 2002, Portugal formally recognized the independence of East Timor, ending centuries of colonial rule and decades of conflict and Indonesian occupation. The small half‑island state stepped into the world map as one of the youngest countries, carrying deep scars and a determined sense of self.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A nation finally claiming its own place on the map mirrors the episode’s quiet reflection on movement, perspective shifts, and finding a sense of home after long journeys.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 2016, despite appeals from Amnesty International and the United Nations, Singapore executed Kho Jabing for a 2008 murder case that had sparked years of legal debate. His hanging drew attention to the city‑state’s steadfast use of capital punishment and reignited global conversations about justice, deterrence, and the value of a single human life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: A stark, irreversible decision around a single person’s fate sits against an episode about pausing and "unfreezing" choices, inviting listeners to reflect on systems that leave no room for second chances.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964, as Penzias and Wilson wrestled with their mysterious antenna noise, they weren’t just hearing the cosmos—they were echoing a long tradition of accidental discoveries in science. Their reluctance to ignore the "hiss" turned what looked like an equipment flaw into one of the strongest proofs that the universe had a fiery beginning.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: The idea of a faint cosmic hum swelling into a foundational insight chimes with an evening mix that starts subtle and slowly crescendos into a dance across worlds.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964, the same background radiation that puzzled engineers turned the sky into a kind of fossil photograph, capturing conditions only 380,000 years after the Big Bang. Today, detailed maps of this microwave afterglow reveal tiny temperature ripples—primordial fingerprints of the galaxies and clusters that would eventually form.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A universe written in faint ripples and cosmic patterns is a perfect companion to an episode explicitly tuned to "cosmic" pulses in dance and electronica.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964, while the cosmos whispered in microwaves, our own planet’s radio chatter was growing denser, knitting Earth into a web of invisible signals. The irony is poetic: at the moment we first heard the universe’s ancient echo, humanity was also becoming louder, filling the spectrum with our own restless transmissions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Contrasting the quiet cosmic background with the noisy human radio spectrum echoes the episode’s exploration of our hyperconnected tech world and the cost of constant signals.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964, the cosmic microwave background’s discovery offered a strange comfort: the night sky isn’t silent at all, it’s filled with the soft after‑ring of creation. Every time we tune across static, we’re brushing against a fragment of that ancient noise, carrying the story of how everything began.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: An almost meditative cosmic hum pairs with an episode about slowing down, inviting listeners to hear the universe itself as a kind of infinite, chilled‑out backdrop.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1964, two radio astronomers at Bell Labs, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, pointed a horn-shaped antenna at the sky and were puzzled by a faint, persistent hiss they couldn’t get rid of. After ruling out everything from equipment noise to pigeon droppings, they realized they had accidentally detected the cosmic microwave background – the afterglow of the Big Bang, a whisper from the birth of the universe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A discovery that turned cosmic "emptiness" into a meaningful glow pairs naturally with an episode about reimagining empty space as creative possibility.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1891, a small group in Thomas Edison's laboratory unveiled the kinetoscope, a peephole device that let one viewer at a time watch a moving film loop. This modest wooden box was the spark that would grow into cinema, transforming light and motion into a new global art form.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: The first flickering films opened a new way to experience time and mood, just as Evening Flows introduces a new format for immersive, cinematic listening.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1714, Johann Sebastian Bach premiered his Pentecost cantata "Erschallet, ihr Lieder" in the chapel of Schloss Weimar. The piece weaves trumpets, strings, and choir into a soaring celebration of spirit, an early glimpse of how Bach would bend complex structures into raw, emotional devotion.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Bach’s sacred cantata and the episode’s focus on powerful vocal presence both center music as spiritual expression that moves hearts beyond time and genre.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1498, Vasco da Gama’s fleet dropped anchor at Kozhikode on India’s Malabar Coast, proving there was a direct sea route from Europe to India. His arrival stitched together distant oceans and trade worlds, carrying spices, stories, and new forms of cultural exchange across the water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An ocean-crossing voyage that rewired world maps resonates with an episode literally set on a boat, celebrating seafaring, journeys, and shared stories on the water.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1497, John Cabot sailed out of Bristol aboard the Matthew, aiming west into largely unknown Atlantic waters in search of a new route to Asia. Instead, he would reach the coasts of North America, adding another unintended chapter to the age of exploration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: Cabot’s westward gamble from a European port echoes the episode’s seaborne tour of Mediterranean cultures, both framing the sea as a corridor of discovery.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1570, cartographer Abraham Ortelius published the "Theatrum Orbis Terrarum," often called the first modern atlas. For the first time, people could flip through a bound book and watch the world take shape in ink, continent by continent, like an early paper interface to the planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: Ortelius bound the world into curated pages much like an iPod playlist; both invite a slower, intentional way of navigating vast information instead of getting lost in endless scrolls.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1932, Amelia Earhart lifted off from Newfoundland in a single‑engine Lockheed Vega, determined to cross the Atlantic alone. Fourteen hours of icing, storms, and exhaustion later, she landed in a farmer’s field in Ireland, becoming the first woman to complete a solo nonstop transatlantic flight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Earhart’s solo leap across the Atlantic mirrors the episode’s celebration of women who step into male‑dominated spaces with loud, unapologetic presence.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1983, a French research team led by Françoise Barré-Sinoussi published the first scientific papers identifying a new retrovirus—later named HIV—as the cause of AIDS. At a time of fear and stigma, their work opened the door to blood tests, antiretroviral therapies, and a new understanding of how a tiny strand of genetic code could reshape societies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A painstaking scientific breakthrough after years of uncertainty fits an episode about how slow, difficult learning can ultimately transform lives.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 2012, a magnitude‑6.0 earthquake struck northern Italy’s Emilia‑Romagna region, toppling historic buildings and damaging warehouses that housed thousands of wheels of Parmigiano Reggiano cheese. Seismologists used the event to study shallow crustal quakes, while local communities rebuilt, turning toppled cheese into a symbol of resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An earthquake that shook everyday life but sparked collective recovery pairs with an episode about weathering storms and holding onto hope through change.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1202, a powerful earthquake ripped through the Levant, its shaking felt from Sicily to Iraq. Medieval chroniclers described cities shattered and fortresses cracked, leaving a rare, haunting record of a tectonic event long before modern seismographs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, far‑reaching jolt from the Earth’s crust aligns metaphorically with an ‘electric shock’ of high‑energy sound reverberating through a morning.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1943, peat cutters near Luttra in Sweden uncovered the remains of a young woman who had died nearly 6,000 years earlier. Preserved by the acidic bog, the Luttra Woman’s body offered a time‑capsule glimpse into Neolithic life and death rituals in northern Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A body sleeping in northern peat for millennia feels akin to gliding through cold, quiet northern waters—both stories of what the Earth preserves in its hidden depths.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1813, Napoleon’s forces met allied Russian and Prussian armies near the town of Bautzen in Saxony. The battle ended in a tactical French victory but failed to destroy the coalition, foreshadowing how even the most aggressive momentum can start to fracture under pressure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A clash of empires and shifting power balances finds a modern echo in a band whose music turns political conflict and resistance into amplified battle cries.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1802, Napoleon Bonaparte signed the Law of 20 May 1802, restoring slavery in France’s Caribbean colonies after it had been abolished during the Revolution. The decision reignited brutal systems of forced labor and sparked resistance that would shape the histories of Haiti, Guadeloupe, and beyond.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A dark turn back toward slavery contrasts sharply with Afrobeat’s later rise as a vehicle for Black liberation and political critique, highlighting how music can answer history’s injustices.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1520, conquistador Hernán Cortés defeated Pánfilo de Narváez at Cempoala, in a battle between two Spanish factions fighting on foreign soil. The clash, meant to rein in Cortés’ insubordination, instead solidified his power and pushed the conquest of the Aztec Empire further forward.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A brutal episode of Spanish colonial expansion connects with Residente’s Latin American critiques of empire, power, and the long shadows of conquest.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1217, the Second Battle of Lincoln saw William Marshal lead English royal forces to defeat Prince Louis of France and rebellious barons. Chroniclers dubbed it the "Fair of Lincoln" because of the vast amount of loot taken, marking a turning point that helped secure the young king Henry III’s throne.

episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd
